title: Handover of Hong Kong to China
summary: At midnight on 1 July 1997, in a rain-drenched ceremony at the Convention and Exhibition Centre in Wan Chai, the Union flag was lowered and the flag of the People's Republic of China raised over Hong Kong, ending 156 years of British colonial rule. Prince Charles read a message from Queen Elizabeth II and departed on the royal yacht Britannia; President Jiang Zemin and Premier Li Peng welcomed the territory back to China. Under the 'one country, two systems' formula agreed with Deng Xiaoping in 1984, Hong Kong became the first Special Administrative Region of the PRC, its capitalist economy and civil liberties promised protection for fifty years.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
British rule in Hong Kong began with the guns of the First Opium War. Under the Treaty of Nanking of 29 August 1842, the Qing empire ceded Hong Kong Island 'in perpetuity' to Queen Victoria. The Convention of Peking of 1860, ending the Second Opium War, added the Kowloon Peninsula. The territory took its final shape with the 1898 Second Convention of Peking, which leased the much larger 'New Territories' north of Kowloon, along with more than two hundred outlying islands, for a term of ninety-nine years. That lease was due to expire at midnight on 30 June 1997.

For most of its colonial history Hong Kong was a modest imperial port, run by a governor and an appointed Executive Council. Its transformation into a global city was compressed into a single generation after the Second World War, when refugees from the Chinese Civil War and, later, from Mao Zedong's Great Leap Forward and Cultural Revolution swelled its population from about 600,000 in 1945 to more than five million by 1980. Light industry, then finance and shipping, made Hong Kong one of the 'Four Asian Tigers' and, by 1997, the world's busiest container port and third-largest financial centre.

As the 1997 expiry approached, London and Beijing faced a practical problem: the leased New Territories were legally and physically inseparable from the ceded portions. Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher, fresh from the Falklands War, opened talks in Beijing in September 1982 by asserting the binding force of the nineteenth-century treaties. Deng Xiaoping, China's paramount leader, rejected them as unequal and insisted on full recovery of sovereignty. He offered a novel bargain: a socialist China would allow a capitalist Hong Kong to preserve its way of life for fifty years, under what he called 'one country, two systems'.

Two years of negotiation produced the Sino-British Joint Declaration, signed by Thatcher and Chinese Premier Zhao Ziyang in Beijing on 19 December 1984 and registered with the United Nations. It promised Hong Kong a 'high degree of autonomy', an independent judiciary applying the common law, and the preservation of existing rights and freedoms, all to be enshrined in a Basic Law that Beijing promulgated on 4 April 1990.

The Tiananmen Square crackdown of 4 June 1989 shook confidence in the arrangement and prompted a wave of emigration; more than half a million Hong Kongers left in the following decade. The last British governor, Chris Patten, appointed in July 1992, introduced modest electoral reforms that expanded the franchise for the 1995 Legislative Council elections. Beijing denounced him as 'a whore for a thousand generations' and vowed to dismantle the reforms after 1997. By the summer of that year the choreography of the handover was set, but its terms were already contested.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The transfer of sovereignty unfolded across the afternoon and midnight of 30 June and the early hours of 1 July 1997. The weather was against the departing power: heavy monsoon rain fell all day. At about 16:30 Hong Kong time Governor Chris Patten received the Union flag, folded to a bugler's last post at Government House, and left the residence for the last time. His three daughters wept publicly. At 18:15, at a sunset ceremony on the parade ground of HMS Tamar, Patten spoke of Hong Kong as a place where 'the Chinese people, on Chinese soil, have lived by British institutions and the rule of law', delivered in a downpour so heavy that his speech notes ran with ink.

Earlier in the afternoon President Jiang Zemin and Premier Li Peng had arrived from Beijing on an Air China Boeing 747. At around 21:00, some 500 vehicles of the People's Liberation Army began moving across the Man Kam To border crossing into the New Territories. The main handover ceremony, attended by roughly 4,000 dignitaries, began at 23:45 in the just-completed Convention and Exhibition Centre extension in Wan Chai, its wing-shaped roof jutting out over Victoria Harbour. The British delegation was led by Prince Charles, representing his mother the Queen, and the newly elected Labour Prime Minister Tony Blair, in office for less than two months.

At 23:59 the band of the Royal Marines played 'God Save the Queen' as the Union flag and the colonial Hong Kong flag were lowered. There followed a deliberate twelve-second pause, at midnight precisely on 1 July 1997 the Chinese national anthem 'March of the Volunteers' struck up, and the flag of the People's Republic of China and the new bauhinia-emblem flag of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region were raised in their place. Sovereignty had passed. Prince Charles, in a private diary later published, described attending 'the Great Chinese Takeaway'.

At around 00:15 the Prince and Chris Patten boarded the royal yacht Britannia at HMS Tamar, joined by British warships as an escort, and sailed out of Victoria Harbour for Manila. It was Britannia's last state voyage. At 01:30 a swearing-in ceremony installed the new government of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region: the shipping magnate Tung Chee-hwa as first Chief Executive, a provisional Legislative Council chosen by a Beijing-selected committee that replaced the elected chamber, and a new Court of Final Appeal. At dawn a fleet of PLA armoured personnel carriers and thousands of soldiers crossed into the territory, taking over British barracks. In Beijing crowds gathered at Tiananmen Square to celebrate; along Nathan Road in Kowloon fireworks lit the harbour on the evening of 1 July.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The transition was smoother than many had feared. Under the Basic Law, Hong Kong retained its own currency, immigration controls, common-law legal system, and civil freedoms, and its passports allowed visa-free travel to jurisdictions where mainland Chinese passports did not. Within weeks, however, the Asian financial crisis of July 1997, which began with the Thai baht's devaluation on 2 July, tested the new order. The Hong Kong Monetary Authority defended the currency peg to the US dollar at the cost of a sharp recession; unemployment rose to a post-war record. Property prices collapsed by more than fifty per cent, wiping out much of the wealth accumulated in the pre-handover boom.

Politically, the story of the first decade after 1997 was one of cautious autonomy. Tung Chee-hwa's proposal in 2003 to implement Article 23 of the Basic Law, requiring anti-subversion legislation, brought half a million people onto the streets on 1 July of that year; the bill was withdrawn, and Tung resigned in 2005. Universal suffrage, promised in the Basic Law as an eventual goal, was repeatedly deferred. In 2014 the National People's Congress ruled that candidates for the Chief Executive would first have to be vetted by a nominating committee, triggering the seventy-nine-day Umbrella Movement protests centred on Admiralty and Mong Kok.

The pressure intensified in the 2010s. A proposed extradition bill in 2019 provoked the largest protests in the city's history, at their peak drawing an estimated two million people, and increasingly violent clashes with police. On 30 June 2020 Beijing bypassed the Hong Kong legislature and imposed a National Security Law criminalising secession, subversion, terrorism, and collusion with foreign forces. Prominent activists were arrested or fled; leading newspapers, notably Apple Daily, were forced to close; and in March 2021 Beijing overhauled the electoral system to ensure that only 'patriots' could stand for office. Whether the 'one country, two systems' formula, promised to endure until 2047, survives in substance is now widely debated.

Britain, for its part, opened a visa route in 2021 for holders of British National (Overseas) passports, granted at handover; more than 150,000 Hong Kongers had moved to the United Kingdom under it by the middle of the decade. Canada, Australia, and Taiwan received smaller waves. The Sino-British Joint Declaration, which Beijing declared a historical document with no continuing force, remains formally in effect according to London and other Western governments.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Hong Kong handover was the largest peaceful transfer of a population, roughly 6.5 million people at the time, from one sovereign state to another in modern history. It marked the effective end of the British Empire; Macao's handover from Portugal in December 1999 was a smaller coda. For China, the return of Hong Kong, and later Macao, was presented as a step toward completing national reunification, with Taiwan the remaining unfinished business.

The 'one country, two systems' formula was designed with Taiwan in mind as much as Hong Kong, an attempt to demonstrate that reunification could preserve local institutions. That demonstration has cut both ways. In its early years it seemed to work: Hong Kong retained its judicial independence, media pluralism, and freewheeling capitalism, while Beijing largely refrained from open interference. In its later years, the erosion of political freedoms and the imposition of the National Security Law have hardened Taiwanese opinion against any similar arrangement, contributing to the electoral dominance of the Democratic Progressive Party.

Economically the handover marked Hong Kong's transition from a British outpost to a Chinese financial hub. Its stock exchange became the venue of choice for mainland Chinese initial public offerings; the offshore renminbi market, launched in 2004, was based in the city. But Shanghai and Shenzhen have grown to rival and in some measures overtake it; the city's share of the Chinese economy, close to twenty per cent at handover, is now under three.

Culturally, the handover generated an ambivalent identity that continues to shape the city's writers, filmmakers, and protesters. Younger Hong Kongers, born after 1997, have shown less attachment to any Chinese national identity than their parents; the umbrella, the yellow ribbon, and the black bauhinia have become symbols of a distinct local politics. What began at midnight on 1 July 1997 as a formal act of decolonisation has proved to be, in its long unfolding, one of the defining tests of how a global city can coexist with a sovereign authoritarian state.

worldContext.text: In July 1997 the United States under President Bill Clinton was enjoying a technology-driven economic boom. On 4 July the NASA Pathfinder mission landed on Mars, and its Sojourner rover began operations two days later. NAFTA was in its fourth year. In Peru, Alberto Fujimori's government continued its authoritarian drift; in Argentina, Carlos Menem's decade of dollarised free-market reform was nearing its end. Canada had just re-elected Jean Chretien's Liberal government in June, and Quebec's separatist movement was regrouping after the narrow 1995 sovereignty referendum defeat.

In May 1997 Mobutu Sese Seko, Zaire's dictator since 1965, had been overthrown by Laurent-Desire Kabila's Rwandan- and Ugandan-backed rebellion; the country was renamed the Democratic Republic of the Congo. Sierra Leone experienced a coup on 25 May 1997 that ousted President Ahmad Tejan Kabbah, deepening its civil war. In South Africa, the Truth and Reconciliation Commission under Archbishop Desmond Tutu was midway through its public hearings. Nigeria remained under the military dictatorship of General Sani Abacha.

India celebrated the fiftieth anniversary of independence from Britain on 15 August 1997, six weeks after the Hong Kong handover, closing another chapter of decolonisation. Pakistan marked the same anniversary; Nawaz Sharif had returned as prime minister in February. In Afghanistan the Taliban had captured Kabul the previous September and were consolidating rule over most of the country; Ahmad Shah Massoud's Northern Alliance held out in the Panjshir. Sri Lanka's civil war continued in its northern provinces.

Iran on 23 May 1997 elected the reformist Mohammad Khatami president in a landslide, briefly raising hopes of political opening. Israeli-Palestinian relations under Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, elected the previous year, had deteriorated after the opening of the Western Wall tunnel; the Hebron Protocol of January 1997 partially implemented the Oslo Accords. Algeria's civil war reached one of its bloodiest phases, with the Rais and Bentalha massacres later that summer. Turkey saw the 'postmodern coup' of 28 February force the resignation of Islamist Prime Minister Necmettin Erbakan.

Australia under John Howard's coalition government was preparing for a national referendum on becoming a republic, ultimately held in 1999 and defeated. The Wik decision of December 1996 on native title continued to shape rural politics. New Zealand under Jim Bolger's National government was moving toward the 'MMP' proportional representation system it had adopted the previous year. In Papua New Guinea, a mercenary contract with Sandline International to fight the Bougainville rebellion triggered a military mutiny and political crisis in March 1997.

figures.name: Jiang Zemin
figures.role: President of the People's Republic of China; received Hong Kong for China

figures.name: Charles, Prince of Wales
figures.role: Representative of Queen Elizabeth II at the handover ceremony (later King Charles III)

figures.name: Chris Patten
figures.role: Last British Governor of Hong Kong, 1992-1997

figures.name: Tony Blair
figures.role: British Prime Minister who inherited the handover, in office for eight weeks at the time

figures.name: Tung Chee-hwa
figures.role: First Chief Executive of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, 1997-2005

figures.name: Deng Xiaoping
figures.role: Chinese paramount leader who negotiated 'one country, two systems'; died in February 1997, months before the handover he had designed

images.caption: The Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre in Wan Chai, whose wing-shaped extension over Victoria Harbour was completed for the handover ceremony of 30 June - 1 July 1997.
The flag of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, featuring a white bauhinia flower on a red field, raised at midnight on 1 July 1997.
images.credit: Design by Tao Ho, via Wikimedia Commons

sources.title: Hong Kong handover: How the world's press covered it
